---
title: "Code Cells"
description: "Run Python and R scripts section by section with code cells in Positron. Use special comments like # %% to divide a script into runnable cells."
---

Code cells let you divide a plain script into sections that you can run one at a time, using special comments like `# %%` as cell boundaries. You get a notebook-like, interactive workflow while your code stays in a regular `.py` or `.R` file that is easy to version, diff, and reuse.

Positron supports code cells in both Python and R files:

-   Cells run in your current [interpreter session](managing-interpreters.qmd), with output shown in the Console, just like running any other code from the editor.
-   The cell containing your cursor is highlighted, and cells display **Run Cell**, **Run Above**, and **Run Next** links above their delimiters.
-   Keyboard shortcuts let you run cells, insert new ones, and move between them without leaving the keyboard.

<!-- TODO(screenshot): images/code-cells-python.png — hero screenshot for this page.

Purpose: first visual a reader sees; must instantly communicate "a plain .py script,
divided into cells, running interactively like a notebook".

Composition:
- Full Positron window, light theme, ~1680x1050 (match images/positron-notebook.png),
  clean profile (no third-party extensions, no notification toasts, no Problems badge).
- Editor: a Python script with 3 cells separated by `# %%` comments. Suggested content
  (matches the code example on this page):
    # %%
    import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
    import pandas as pd
    penguins = pd.read_csv("penguins.csv")
    penguins.head()
    # %%
    penguins.groupby("species")["body_mass_g"].mean()
    # %%
    penguins.plot.scatter(x="flipper_length_mm", y="body_mass_g")
    plt.title("Penguin flipper length vs. body mass")
    plt.show()
  (imports in the first cell so Ruff does not flag E402 and the Problems bar stays clean)
- Cursor placed in the SECOND cell so its background highlight is visible mid-file and it
  shows the full CodeLens row: Run Cell | Run Above | Run Next.
- Console: output of the first two cells visible (head() rows + groupby means), Python
  session active; multi-session list (Python + R) visible is a nice bonus.
- Plots pane: the scatter plot rendered.
- Variables pane: penguins DataFrame listed.

When the image is added, restore this embed:

![A Python script in Positron divided into code cells, with the active cell highlighted, Run Cell links above each cell, and output in the Console.](images/code-cells-python.png){fig-alt="Positron IDE showing a Python script with several code cells separated by cell delimiter comments. The cell containing the cursor is highlighted, Run Cell links appear above each cell, and the Console pane shows the output of the last cell."}
-->

::: {.callout-note}
Code cells are a feature for plain script files. To work with notebook files instead, see [Jupyter Notebooks](jupyter-notebooks.qmd).
:::

## Creating code cells

Start a new cell by adding a comment line that begins with the cell delimiter. A cell extends until the next delimiter or the end of the file. You can also use the _Positron: Insert Code Cell_ command or {{< kbd mac=Command-Shift-I win=Ctrl-Shift-I linux=Ctrl-Shift-I >}} to insert a new cell below the current one.

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Python

In Python files, a comment starting with `# %%` begins a new cell:

```python
# %%
import pandas as pd

penguins = pd.read_csv("penguins.csv")
penguins.head()

# %%
penguins.groupby("species")["body_mass_g"].mean()
```

This is the same ["percent" format](https://jupytext.org/formats/scripts/#the-percent-format) used by Jupytext, Spyder, and VS Code, so scripts with code cells move between tools without changes. Markdown cells in this format, starting with `# %% [markdown]`, are also supported; running one renders its contents as formatted output in the Console.

### R

In R files, a comment starting with `# %%` or `# +` begins a new cell:

```r
# %%
penguins <- read.csv("penguins.csv")
head(penguins)

# %%
aggregate(body_mass_g ~ species, data = penguins, FUN = mean)
```

A [section header comment](https://docs.posit.co/ide/user/ide/guide/code/code-sections.html) such as `# Load data ----` ends the current cell, so you can combine code sections and code cells in the same script.

<!-- TODO(screenshot): images/code-cells-r.png — R companion screenshot.

Purpose: show that code cells are not Python-only; placed right after the R example so
the reader sees the same `# %%` workflow in an .R file, with output flowing to the R
console and the Plots pane.

Composition:
- Full Positron window, light theme, ~1680x1050, clean profile (same setup as the
  Python screenshot for visual consistency).
- Editor: an R script with 3 cells separated by `# %%` comments. Suggested content
  (matches the code example on this page):
    # %%
    penguins <- read.csv("penguins.csv")
    head(penguins)
    # %%
    aggregate(body_mass_g ~ species, data = penguins, FUN = mean)
    # %%
    plot(penguins$flipper_length_mm, penguins$body_mass_g,
         xlab = "Flipper length (mm)", ylab = "Body mass (g)",
         main = "Penguin flipper length vs. body mass")
- Cursor in one of the cells so the active-cell highlight and its CodeLens links
  (Run Cell | Run Above | Run Next) are visible.
- Console: R session active showing head() and aggregate() output.
- Plots pane: the base-R scatter plot rendered.

When the image is added, restore this embed:

![An R script in Positron divided into code cells, with output from each cell in the Console and a plot in the Plots pane.](images/code-cells-r.png){fig-alt="Positron IDE showing an R script divided into code cells, with the active cell highlighted, Run Cell links above each cell, cell output in the Console pane, and a scatter plot in the Plots pane."}
-->

:::

### Custom cell delimiters

To use an additional cell delimiter, set [`codeCells.additionalCellDelimiter`](positron://settings/codeCells.additionalCellDelimiter). By default, this setting adds support for the `# COMMAND ----------` delimiter used by Databricks notebooks exported as source files.

## Running code cells

Click the **Run Cell** link above a cell to run it in your current Console session. The **Run Above** link runs all cells before the current cell, which is useful for restoring your session state from the top of the script, and **Run Next** runs the cell that follows.

You can also run cells with keyboard shortcuts:

| Shortcut | Description |
| -------- | ----------- |
| {{< kbd mac=Command-Shift-Enter win=Ctrl-Shift-Enter linux=Ctrl-Shift-Enter >}} | Run the current cell |
| {{< kbd Shift-Enter >}} | Run the current cell and advance to the next cell |
| {{< kbd mac=Command-Alt-R win=Ctrl-Alt-R linux=Ctrl-Alt-R >}} | Run all cells |
| {{< kbd mac=Command-Shift-Alt-P win=Ctrl-Shift-Alt-P linux=Ctrl-Shift-Alt-P >}} | Run all cells above the current cell |
| {{< kbd mac=Command-Shift-Alt-N win=Ctrl-Shift-Alt-N linux=Ctrl-Shift-Alt-N >}} | Run all cells below the current cell |
| {{< kbd mac=Command-Alt-P win=Ctrl-Alt-P linux=Ctrl-Alt-P >}} | Run the previous cell |
| {{< kbd mac=Command-Alt-N win=Ctrl-Alt-N linux=Ctrl-Alt-N >}} | Run the next cell |

All of these commands are also available from the [Command Palette](command-palette.qmd), such as _Positron: Run All Cells_.

## Navigating between cells

Move between cells without running them using {{< kbd mac=Command-PageDown win=Ctrl-PageDown linux=Ctrl-PageDown >}} to go to the next cell and {{< kbd mac=Command-PageUp win=Ctrl-PageUp linux=Ctrl-PageUp >}} to go to the previous cell, or the corresponding _Positron: Go to Next Cell_ and _Positron: Go to Previous Cell_ commands.

## Customizing cell appearance

The cell containing your cursor is highlighted with a background color by default. Use [`codeCells.cellStyle`](positron://settings/codeCells.cellStyle) to choose a `background`, `border`, or `both` style for the active cell. The colors come from your theme: customize them via the `notebook.selectedCellBackground` and `interactive.activeCodeBorder` [theme colors](https://code.visualstudio.com/api/references/theme-color).
